elements: 2D grid with cubes representing sampling points
annotated text notes on 1D and 2D methods

interactions.label: 1D/2D View
interactions.teaches: Switching between a 1D line plot and a 2D grid to visualize interpolation in different dimensions.

interactions.label: Position A
interactions.teaches: Shows nearest input value selection as you change horizontal position on the 1D plot.

interactions.label: Position A,B
interactions.teaches: Shows nearest neighbor selection by adjusting position on the 2D grid.

demo_steps.title: 1D Interpolation Basics
demo_steps.narration: We're starting in the 1D view. Moving position A across the line, you can see how the highlighted point jumps between discrete input values. This illustrates the piecewise-constant nature of nearest neighbor interpolation.

demo_steps.title: Transition to 2D View
demo_steps.narration: Switching to the 2D view now. This grid represents our 2D input space, where each cube is a potential sample point. Use position A,B to explore how the nearest value is chosen based on proximity.

demo_steps.title: Insight on Interpolation Strategy
demo_steps.narration: Notice how in both dimensions, the selected output is the nearest input value causing abrupt transitions. These characteristics have implications for applications like image resizing or signal processing.

plan.camera_suggestion: Start with a view focused on the 1D line plot, then smoothly transition to the 2D grid as the toggle is switched.
plan.notes: Ensure seamless transition animations between the 1D and 2D views with correct scaling for both line markers and 2D cube elements.

plan.contextChunk:
can use different interpolations functions as we will discuss next.

### Nearest Neighbor Interpolation {#sec-interpolation}

In **nearest neighbor interpolation** we assign to
$\ell_{\texttt{out}}(a,b)$ the closest value of the input
$\ell_{\texttt{in}}$:
$$\ell_{\texttt{out}}(a,b) = \ell_{\texttt{in}}[ n, m ]$$ where
$n-0.5<a<n+0.5$ and $m-0.5<b<m+0.5$. Note that here we are treating
$\ell_{\texttt{out}}(a,b)$ as a continuous image. Nearest neighbor
interpolation results in an image that is piecewise-constant.
@fig-upsampling_and_downsampling-nn_interp shows the nearest neighbor
interpolation process graphically.


![Nearest neighbor interpolation in 1D and 2D.](figures/upsamplig_downsampling/nearest_interp2.png){#fig-upsampling_and_downsampling-nn_interp width=100%}


### Linear and Bilinear Interpolation {#sec-bilinearinterpolation}

For 1D signals, the value of
$\ell_{\texttt{out}}(a)$ is a result of linear interpolation between the
two closest input values $\ell_{\texttt{in}}[n]$ and
$\ell_{\texttt{in}}[n+1]$, where $n<a<n+1$. 


:::{.column-margin}
Interpolation is also a key operation when performing image warping.
:::

For 2D signals, we have to

interactions.label: Position A
interactions.teaches: Adjusting horizontal position to see which value is selected in 1D interpolation.

interactions.label: Position A,B
interactions.teaches: Adjusting position in the 2D plane to see which value is selected in 2D interpolation.

demo_steps.title: Understanding 1D Interpolation
demo_steps.narration: Here you see a 1D nearest neighbor interpolation. As you move position A, notice how the output immediately jumps between the input values, demonstrating piecewise-constant behavior.

demo_steps.title: Exploring 2D Neighbor Selection
demo_steps.narration: Now, in the 2D view, adjusting the position over the grid, you observe how the closest input grid value is chosen for the output. This highlights the nearest neighbor strategy in two dimensions.

demo_steps.title: Key Insight on Interpolation
demo_steps.narration: Notice how nearest neighbor interpolation selects the input value closest to the output position, which can cause abrupt changes. This is useful in scenarios where smoothness is not required.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Initial view should focus on the 1D line plot and grid for 2D, default zoom to capture whole scene clearly.
interactionPlan.notes: The toggle must smoothly transition between views, and sliders manipulate corresponding positions accurately.
